content.text: What Single Sign-On Means in Entra ID

Single Sign-On (SSO) in Microsoft Entra ID means a user authenticates once and gains access to multiple applications without re-entering credentials. Entra achieves this by setting a persistent session cookie (ESTSAUTH) in the browser after the first authentication. Subsequent authorization requests to Entra ID — from any application — are fulfilled silently if that cookie is present and valid.

SSO is a property of the Entra ID session, not of any individual application. When an application redirects a user to /authorize, Entra evaluates whether an active session already exists. If it does, it issues tokens immediately without showing a login prompt. The user experiences this as seamless navigation between applications.

attrs.alt: SSO session flow: first authentication sets ESTSAUTH cookie, second app gets tokens silently

content.text: SSO Modes for Enterprise Applications

When you configure an enterprise application in Entra ID, you select its SSO mode. The mode determines the protocol and mechanism by which Entra issues authentication to that specific application. The four modes are not all equivalent — SAML and OIDC are federation-based (Entra is the identity provider), while Password SSO and Linked SSO involve no Entra-issued tokens reaching the application.
SSO Mode
Protocol
Token type
Best for
SAML
SAML 2.0 / WS-Fed
Signed XML assertion
Enterprise apps with SAML SP support (Salesforce, SAP, etc.)
OpenID Connect
OAuth 2.0 + OIDC
JWT access + ID token
Modern web and API apps registered in Entra
Password-based
None (credential vault)
None — browser extension fills form
Legacy apps with username/password forms only
Linked
None (redirect)
None — external URL only
Apps already protected by another IdP, listed in My Apps

Prefer SAML or OIDC over Password SSO. Password SSO stores credentials in the tenant and relies on a browser extension, which creates a dependency on Intune or the My Apps Secure Sign-in extension. It provides no Conditional Access enforcement and no MFA capability at token level.
OIDC SSO: Seamless Browser Session Reuse

For OIDC applications built on MSAL, SSO works through two complementary mechanisms: the Entra ID session cookie (for browser-based flows) and the MSAL token cache (for in-process silent token acquisition). When an MSAL application calls acquire_token_silent, it first checks its own cache before attempting a network call.

# MSAL SSO: silent token acquisition from cache
from msal import PublicClientApplication

# Public client (desktop/mobile): uses persistent token cache
app = PublicClientApplication(
    client_id='<client-id>',
    authority='https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>',
    # token_cache is in-memory by default; use SerializableTokenCache for disk persistence
)

# Try silent acquisition first (uses refresh token if access token is expired)
accounts = app.get_accounts()
result = None
if accounts:
    result = app.acquire_token_silent(
        scopes=['User.Read'],
        account=accounts[0]   # use first cached account
    )

if not result:
    # No cached token — interactive sign-in required
    result = app.acquire_token_interactive(
        scopes=['User.Read'],
        # prompt='none' attempts SSO without showing UI; fails if no session
        # prompt='select_account' forces account picker
    )

if 'access_token' in result:
    print('Token acquired for:', result.get('id_token_claims', {}).get('preferred_username'))
else:
    print('Error:', result.get('error'), result.get('error_description'))

# Persistent cache: serialize after sign-in, deserialize on startup
from msal import SerializableTokenCache
import os

CACHE_FILE = os.path.expanduser('~/.msal_cache.bin')
cache = SerializableTokenCache()
if os.path.exists(CACHE_FILE):
    cache.deserialize(open(CACHE_FILE, 'r').read())

app2 = PublicClientApplication('<client-id>', authority='https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>', token_cache=cache)

# After any token operation, save if dirty
if cache.has_state_changed:
    open(CACHE_FILE, 'w').write(cache.serialize())
SAML SSO: SP-Initiated vs IdP-Initiated Flow

SAML SSO has two entry points. In SP-initiated SSO, the user starts at the application (SP), which issues a SAMLRequest and redirects to Entra ID's SAML endpoint. In IdP-initiated SSO, the user starts from the My Apps portal or a deep link — Entra ID sends an unsolicited SAMLResponse directly to the application's Assertion Consumer Service URL. SP-initiated is the recommended flow because it allows the SP to include a relay state (return URL) and reduces the risk of CSRF-style attacks with forged assertions.

# Retrieve SAML SSO configuration for an enterprise app via Graph
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Application.Read.All'

$spId = '<service-principal-object-id>'
$sp   = Get-MgServicePrincipal -ServicePrincipalId $spId

# Basic SAML endpoints
[PSCustomObject]@{
    EntityId          = $sp.IdentifierUris -join ', '
    ReplyUrls         = $sp.ReplyUrls -join ', '
    LoginUrl          = $sp.LoginUrl
    LogoutUrl         = $sp.LogoutUrl
    PreferredSSO      = $sp.PreferredSingleSignOnMode
    SAMLMetadataUrl   = "https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/federationmetadata/2007-06/federationmetadata.xml?appid=$($sp.AppId)"
} | Format-List

# Entra ID's SAML endpoints (tenant-specific)
$tenantId = (Get-MgOrganization).Id
[PSCustomObject]@{
    SingleSignOnService  = "https://login.microsoftonline.com/$tenantId/saml2"
    SingleLogoutService  = "https://login.microsoftonline.com/$tenantId/saml2"
    IdPEntityId          = "https://sts.windows.net/$tenantId/"
    SigningCertEndpoint  = "https://login.microsoftonline.com/$tenantId/federationmetadata/2007-06/federationmetadata.xml"
}
Session Controls and Sign-In Frequency

Conditional Access session controls modify how long an SSO session is valid and whether Entra re-authenticates the user. Sign-in frequency sets a maximum time before the user must re-authenticate, regardless of browser session. Persistent browser session controls whether Entra issues a persistent session cookie that survives browser restarts.
Session control
Effect on SSO
Sign-in frequency (time-based)
User must re-authenticate after N hours/days; SSO cookie is invalidated
Sign-in frequency (every time)
No SSO between sessions; user re-auths on every browser session
Persistent browser: never
Session cookie expires with browser; no SSO after restart on shared devices
Persistent browser: always
Session persists across browser restarts (appropriate for personal devices)
Continuous Access Evaluation (CAE)
Real-time revocation: apps supporting CAE get notified within ~15min of policy change
Single Logout (SLO)

Single Logout ensures that signing out of one application terminates the Entra ID session and optionally notifies all other applications the user has visited. Entra ID supports two SLO mechanisms: front-channel (redirect-based, sends logout requests to each SP via the browser) and back-channel (OIDC back-channel logout, direct HTTP POST from Entra to each app's logout endpoint).

# OIDC logout with Entra ID
# Step 1: Clear local session
# Step 2: Redirect to Entra logout endpoint to clear SSO cookie
import urllib.parse
from flask import session, redirect

TENANT_ID     = '<tenant-id>'
POST_LOGOUT   = 'https://app.example.com/'  # where to land after logout

@flask_app.route('/logout')
def logout():
    session.clear()  # clear local application session
    logout_url = (
        f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/logout'
        + '?' + urllib.parse.urlencode({'post_logout_redirect_uri': POST_LOGOUT})
    )
    return redirect(logout_url)

# OIDC Back-channel logout endpoint (receives logout tokens from Entra)
# Register this URI in the App Registration > Authentication > Front-channel logout URL
import jwt

@flask_app.route('/backchannel-logout', methods=['POST'])
def backchannel_logout():
    logout_token = request.form.get('logout_token')
    if not logout_token:
        return 'Missing logout_token', 400
    # Validate the logout token (signed JWT from Entra)
    # Must verify: iss, aud, iat, jti, sub or sid, events claim
    try:
        jwks_uri = f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/discovery/v2.0/keys'
        jwks_client = jwt.PyJWKClient(jwks_uri)
        signing_key = jwks_client.get_signing_key_from_jwt(logout_token)
        claims = jwt.decode(
            logout_token,
            signing_key.key,
            algorithms=['RS256'],
            audience='<client-id>'
        )
        # Verify 'events' claim contains back-channel logout event
        if 'http://schemas.openid.net/event/backchannel-logout' not in claims.get('events', {}):
            return 'Invalid logout token events', 400
        # Invalidate all sessions for this sub/sid
        invalidate_user_sessions(sub=claims.get('sub'), sid=claims.get('sid'))
        return '', 200
    except Exception as e:
        return f'Token validation error: {e}', 400

def invalidate_user_sessions(sub, sid):
    # Placeholder: remove session from your session store
    print(f'Logging out sub={sub} sid={sid}')
Troubleshooting SSO Failures

SSO failures surface as either a login prompt when one should not appear (silent auth failed) or an error page after redirect. The My Sign-ins portal (mysignins.microsoft.com) and the Entra audit log sign-in entries are the primary diagnostic tools.

# Query sign-in logs to diagnose SSO failures
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'AuditLog.Read.All'

# Find failed sign-ins for a specific user in the last 24 hours
$since = (Get-Date).AddHours(-24).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')
$upn   = 'alice@contoso.com'

Get-MgAuditLogSignIn `
    -Filter "userPrincipalName eq '$upn' and status/errorCode ne 0 and createdDateTime ge $since" `
    -Top 50 |
    Select-Object CreatedDateTime, AppDisplayName,
        @{N='ErrorCode';   E={$_.Status.ErrorCode}},
        @{N='Reason';      E={$_.Status.FailureReason}},
        @{N='AuthMethod';  E={$_.AuthenticationDetails[0]?.AuthenticationMethod}},
        @{N='CAOutcome';   E={$_.ConditionalAccessStatus}},
        @{N='IPAddress';   E={$_.IpAddress}} |
    Format-Table -AutoSize

# Check if a session cookie was present (isInteractive = false means SSO succeeded)
Get-MgAuditLogSignIn `
    -Filter "userPrincipalName eq '$upn' and createdDateTime ge $since" `
    -Top 20 |
    Select-Object CreatedDateTime, AppDisplayName, IsInteractive,
        @{N='TokenIssuerType';E={$_.TokenIssuerType}},
        @{N='SSOApplied';     E={$_.SsoExtensionVersion}} |
    Format-Table
Symptom
Likely cause
Fix
Login prompt appears despite active session
CA sign-in frequency policy or session expired
Check CA policies targeting the app; review session lifetime settings
AADSTS50058: silent auth failed
No SSO cookie in request (third-party cookie blocked)
Use prompt=login or configure SameSite/cross-origin allowlist
AADSTS700054: response_type id_token not enabled
Implicit grant not enabled in App Registration
Enable ID token implicit grant in Authentication settings
SAML ACS receives no assertion
Reply URL mismatch or certificate expired
Verify ACS URL and renew signing certificate in Entra portal
Loop between app and Entra login page
App not consuming auth code; redirect loop
Check callback handler; verify client secret/cert is valid
SSO for Desktop and Mobile Applications

Desktop and mobile applications access SSO differently from web apps. On Windows, the Microsoft Authentication Library uses Windows Account Manager (WAM) as the broker — WAM communicates with the operating system's identity store to acquire tokens silently without any browser redirect. On macOS and iOS, the Microsoft Enterprise SSO plug-in enables SSO across Safari and native apps from a single authentication.

# MSAL on desktop: SSO via WAM broker (Windows) or system browser with cache
from msal import PublicClientApplication, SerializableTokenCache
import json, os

CACHE_FILE = os.path.expanduser('~/.config/myapp/token_cache.json')

def build_cache():
    cache = SerializableTokenCache()
    if os.path.exists(CACHE_FILE):
        with open(CACHE_FILE) as f:
            cache.deserialize(f.read())
    return cache

def save_cache(cache):
    if cache.has_state_changed:
        os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(CACHE_FILE), exist_ok=True)
        with open(CACHE_FILE, 'w') as f:
            f.write(cache.serialize())

def get_token(scopes):
    cache = build_cache()
    app = PublicClientApplication(
        client_id='<client-id>',
        authority='https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>',
        token_cache=cache,
        # On Windows, enable WAM broker for silent SSO:
        # enable_broker_on_windows=True  (MSAL 1.20+)
    )
    accounts = app.get_accounts()
    result = None
    if accounts:
        # Try cache / refresh token first
        result = app.acquire_token_silent(scopes, account=accounts[0])
    if not result:
        # Fall back to interactive — opens system browser
        result = app.acquire_token_interactive(
            scopes=scopes,
            # If a Microsoft account is already signed in to the OS,
            # the browser may complete without any user input (SSO)
        )
    save_cache(cache)
    if 'error' in result:
        raise RuntimeError(f"{result['error']}: {result.get('error_description')}")
    return result['access_token']

# Usage
token = get_token(['https://graph.microsoft.com/User.Read'])
print('Token starts:', token[:20])
Platform
SSO mechanism
Requirement
Windows 10/11 (domain-joined)
WAM broker + PRT (Primary Refresh Token)
Hybrid or Entra-joined device; MSAL with broker enabled
Windows (workgroup)
MSAL token cache + system browser
User signs in once; cache persists across app restarts
macOS
Enterprise SSO plugin in Intune MDM profile
Company Portal installed; plugin deployed via MDM
iOS
Microsoft Authenticator or Company Portal as broker
App uses MSAL with broker flag; broker installed
Android
Microsoft Authenticator as broker
App uses MSAL with broker; Authenticator installed

The Primary Refresh Token (PRT) on Windows is a long-lived credential (14 days, renewable) that Entra issues to Entra-joined or hybrid-joined devices. WAM uses the PRT to acquire access tokens silently without any user interaction — this is the most seamless SSO experience possible for Windows users.
Seamless SSO for On-Premises and Hybrid Environments

Azure AD Seamless SSO (also called Azure AD Seamless Single Sign-On) enables users on domain-joined machines that cannot reach a domain controller — such as remote workers — to authenticate silently to Entra ID using their Windows credentials. It works by deploying a computer account in Active Directory and publishing a Kerberos service principal that Entra ID can validate.

# Check Seamless SSO status and roll over the Kerberos decryption key
# Run on an AD Connect server

Import-Module AzureADSSO
New-AzureADSSOAuthenticationContext

# Check current status across all domains
Get-AzureADSSOStatus | Select-Object Domains, TenantId, IsSeamlessSSOEnabled

# Enable Seamless SSO on new forest/domain
Enable-AzureADSSO -Enable $true

# Roll over the Kerberos decryption key (recommended every 30 days)
# The key is a shared secret between the AZUREADSSOACC computer account and Entra ID
Update-AzureADSSOForest -OnPremCredentials (Get-Credential) -PreserveCustomPermissionsOnDesktopSsoAccount

# Verify Seamless SSO works for a specific UPN
# The user's browser must send a Kerberos ticket to:
# https://autologon.microsoftazuread-sso.com
# This URL must be in the 'Intranet' zone in IE/Edge group policy

# GPO path: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates >
#   Windows Components > Internet Explorer > Internet Control Panel >
#   Security Page > Site to Zone Assignment List
# Add: autologon.microsoftazuread-sso.com = 1 (Intranet zone)
Testing SSO Flows End-to-End

Before deploying SSO to production users, test each protocol path using Entra's built-in test tools and direct protocol traces. The Entra portal's 'Test single sign-on' feature sends a synthetic SAML or OIDC request and shows the decoded assertion or token response, making it the fastest way to verify attribute mapping and signing certificate correctness.

# Automated SSO health check using Microsoft Graph
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Application.Read.All','AuditLog.Read.All'

# Get all SAML-configured enterprise apps
$samlApps = Get-MgServicePrincipal `
    -Filter "preferredSingleSignOnMode eq 'saml'" `
    -Property 'id,displayName,loginUrl,replyUrls,keyCredentials' `
    -All

# Report: SAML apps with expiring or missing signing certificates
$now = Get-Date
$threshold = $now.AddDays(60)

Write-Output '=== SAML apps with expiring certificates (next 60 days) ==='
$samlApps | ForEach-Object {
    $sp = $_
    $certs = Get-MgServicePrincipalTokenSigningCertificate -ServicePrincipalId $sp.Id -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
    $activeCerts = $certs | Where-Object { $_.Status -eq 'Active' }
    if (-not $activeCerts) {
        Write-Warning "$($sp.DisplayName): NO active signing certificate"
    } else {
        $activeCerts | Where-Object { $_.EndDateTime -lt $threshold } |
            Select-Object @{N='App';E={$sp.DisplayName}},
                          DisplayName, EndDateTime, Thumbprint
    }
} | Format-Table

Write-Output ''
Write-Output '=== SAML apps with no recent sign-ins (last 30 days) ==='
$since = $now.AddDays(-30).ToString('yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ssZ')
$activeAppIds = (Get-MgAuditLogSignIn -Filter "createdDateTime ge $since" -Top 5000 |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty AppId -Unique)
$samlApps | Where-Object { $_.AppId -notin $activeAppIds } |
    Select-Object DisplayName, LoginUrl | Format-Table
Summary

SSO in Microsoft Entra ID is session-based: one authentication sets the ESTSAUTH cookie, and subsequent application redirects receive tokens silently. The SSO mode configured on each enterprise application determines the protocol — SAML for federated enterprise apps, OIDC for modern applications — while Conditional Access session controls govern how long SSO sessions remain valid.
SSO works via the ESTSAUTH browser session cookie set after first authentication — no per-app re-authentication needed
MSAL implements SSO through its token cache: acquire_token_silent checks the cache before any network call
SAML SSO uses signed XML assertions; SP-initiated flow is preferred over IdP-initiated for CSRF protection
Sign-in frequency CA controls override SSO: users must re-authenticate after the configured interval
Single Logout should redirect to /oauth2/v2.0/logout to clear the Entra session, not just the app session
Back-channel logout tokens are signed JWTs; validate issuer, audience, and the events claim before invalidating sessions
isInteractive=false in sign-in logs confirms SSO succeeded; AADSTS50058 indicates the session cookie was absent
